name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱقْتَرَبَتِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ وَٱنشَقَّ ٱلْقَمَرُ (١)
```

```english
The Hour has drawn near and the moon is split. (1)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ ءَايَةً يُعْرِضُوا۟ وَيَقُولُوا۟ سِحْرٌ مُّسْتَمِرٌّ (٢)
```

```english
If they see a sign, they turn away, and say, ‘An incessant magic!’ (2)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَّبُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ (٣)
```

```english
They denied and followed their own desires, and every matter has a setting [appropriate to it]. (3)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَنۢبَآءِ مَا فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ (٤)
```

```english
There have already come to them reports containing admonishment, (4)
```

```arabic
حِكْمَةٌۢ بَـٰلِغَةٌ ۖ فَمَا تُغْنِ ٱلنُّذُرُ (٥)
```

```english
[and representing] far-reaching wisdom; but warnings are of no avail! (5)
```

```arabic
فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ۘ يَوْمَ يَدْعُ ٱلدَّاعِ إِلَىٰ شَىْءٍ نُّكُرٍ (٦)
```

```english
So turn away from them! The day when the Caller calls to a dire thing, (6)
```

```arabic
خُشَّعًا أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرَادٌ مُّنتَشِرٌ (٧)
```

```english
they will emerge from the graves as if they were scattered locusts with a humbled look [in their eyes], (7)
```

```arabic
مُّهْطِعِينَ إِلَى ٱلدَّاعِ ۖ يَقُولُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ (٨)
```

```english
scrambling toward the summoner. The faithless will say, ‘This is a hard day!’ (8)
```

## (Commentary)

This Surah is entirely dedicated to warning (الإنذار) and intimidation (التخويف), except for two verses at its end which give glad tidings to the God-conscious (المتقين) of Paradise and presence with their Lord.

The Surah begins by referring to the sign (الآية) of the splitting of the moon, which the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought forth upon the proposal of his people. It mentions their accusing him of sorcery, their denial of him, and their following of whims (الأهواء) despite the deterrent news (الأنباء الزاجرة) that had come to them regarding the Day of Resurrection and the accounts of past destroyed nations. Then, the Exalted repeats to them a portion of those accounts in a tone of indignation and reproach, describing their wretched state on the Day of Resurrection when they emerge from the graves (الأجداث) and present themselves for the reckoning (الحساب).

Next, it points to the stories of the people of Nuh, ‘Ad, Thamud, the people of Lut, and the folk of Pharaoh, and the painful punishment that befell them following their denial of the warnings (النذر). The people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) are not more dear to Allah than they were, nor can they escape Him. The Surah concludes with glad tidings for the God-conscious.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses. No weight is given to the claim that it was revealed at Badr, nor to the claim that some of its verses are Medinan. Among its most luminous verses are those concerning predestination (القدر) at its end.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ** — *The Hour has drawn near and the moon is split.* (54:1). **Approaching (الاقتراب)** denotes an increase in nearness; thus, His saying: **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ** means it has become very near. The Hour (الساعة) is the temporal vessel in which the Resurrection occurs.

His saying: **وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ** means its parts separated from one another, becoming two distinct pieces or halves. The verse refers to the miracle of the splitting of the moon (شق القمر) which Allah the Exalted performed at the hand of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), following a request from the polytheists among the people of Mecca. Narrations regarding this are widespread (مستفيضة), and the scholars of Hadith and exegetes are in agreement regarding its acceptance, as has been stated. None among them disagreed except al-Hasan, ‘Ata, and al-Balkhi, who said: The meaning of His saying **انْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ** is that the moon *will* split at the onset of the Hour, and the past tense was used only to emphasize the certainty of its occurrence.

This view is refuted and dismissed by the evidence of the following verse: **وَإِنْ يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَيَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ** — *If they see a sign, they turn away and say, ‘An incessant magic!’* (54:2). The context is the clearest witness that the word "sign (آية)" is general, encompassing the splitting of the moon. Upon its occurrence, they turned away and said, "An incessant magic." It is well known that the Day of Resurrection is a day when realities are manifest and people are compelled toward knowledge (المعرفة); there would be no meaning then for them to say of a manifest sign that it is "incessant magic." Thus, it can only be a sign that occurred to demonstrate truth and veracity, yet they chose to dismiss it out of stubbornness by labeling it sorcery.

Equally baseless is the claim that the verse refers to what modern astronomers have suggested—that the moon is a piece of the earth, just as the earth is a part separated from the sun. According to this view, His saying **وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ** refers to a scientific fact that had not yet been discovered at the time of revelation.

This is because this theory, even if assumed to be correct, does not align with His saying: **وَإِنْ يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَيَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ**, as it has never been reported that anyone said of the moon [as a celestial body] that it is "incessant magic."

Furthermore, the separation of the moon from the earth would be termed *ishtiqaq* (اشتقاق), whereas what is in the noble verse is *inshiqaq* (انشقاق). The term *inshiqaq* is only applied to a single thing breaking into two pieces within itself, not to its separation from another thing of which it was once a part.

Similarly baseless is the claim that the splitting of the moon means the dispelling of darkness upon its rising, or the claim that it is a metaphor for the manifestation of the matter and the clarity of the truth.

The verse is not without an indication that the splitting of the moon is among the portents of the approach of the Hour.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَيَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ** — *If they see a sign, they turn away and say, ‘An incessant magic!’* (54:2). **Continuance (الاستمرار)** of a thing is its passing through time again and again; hence it is applied to that which is perpetual and consistent. Thus, their saying "incessant magic" means one magic after another, occurring continuously.

The word "sign (آية)" is an indefinite noun in a conditional context, thereby implying generality. The meaning is: every sign they witness, they say of it that it is magic following magic. Some have interpreted "incessant (المستمر)" as that which is firm and strong, others as that which is passing and vanishing, and others as that which is hideous and repulsive—but these are far-fetched meanings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَذَّبُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُسْتَقِرٌّ** — *They denied and followed their own desires, and every matter has a [fixed] settlement.* (54:3). The object of the denial, by the context of the end of the verse, is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the signs he brought. That is: they denied the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the signs he brought, while the reality is that every matter will eventually settle in its place of rest (مستقره), whereby it will be known whether it was truth or falsehood, sincerity or a lie. Thus, they will come to know whether the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was truthful or a liar, and whether he was upon the truth or not. So His saying: **وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُسْتَقِرٌّ** carries the same meaning as His saying: **وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُ بَعْدَ حِينٍ** — *And you will surely come to know its tidings after a while.* (38:88).

It has been said that the object of denial is the splitting of the moon, meaning: they denied the splitting of the moon and followed their whims. However, the sentence **وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُسْتَقِرٌّ** does not suit that interpretation as perfectly.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْباءِ ما فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ** — *There has already come to them such news as contains a deterrent,* (54:4). **Deterrence (المزدجر)** is a mim-infinitive meaning taking heed (الاتعاظ). His saying **مِنَ الْأَنْباءِ** (of the news) is an explanation of what contains the deterrent. What is meant by "news" are the accounts of the past destroyed nations or the news of the Day of Resurrection; both are possible. What is apparent from the verse being followed by the news of the Day of Resurrection and then the news of several destroyed nations is that the "news containing a deterrent" refers to all of that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حِكْمَةٌ بالِغَةٌ فَما تُغْنِ النُّذُرُ** — *profound wisdom; but warnings are of no avail.* (54:5). **Wisdom (الحكمة)** is the word of truth from which benefit is derived. **Reaching (البلوغ)** is the arrival of a thing at the end of its distance, and it is used metaphorically for the completeness and perfection of a thing. Thus, "profound wisdom (حكمة بالغة)" is the complete and perfect wisdom in which there is no deficiency, neither in itself nor in its effect.

His saying: **فَما تُغْنِ النُّذُرُ** — the "fa" here is *fasihah* (فصيحة), disclosing an elided sentence upon which the speech is predicated. The word *nudhur* (النذر) is the plural of *nadhir*, meaning either the warner (المنذر) or the warning (الإنذار); both are correct, though the former is more readily understood.

The meaning is: This Quran, or that to which they are called, is profound wisdom which they denied while following their whims; so what avail are the warners or the warnings?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ يَدْعُ الدَّاعِ إِلى شَيْءٍ نُكُرٍ** — *So leave them alone. The day the Summoner summons to a dire thing,* (54:6). Turning away (التولي) is to disregard. The "fa" in **فَتَوَلَّ** makes the command to turn away a consequence of the preceding description of their state. That is: since they are deniers of you, followers of their whims, for whom warnings are of no avail and deterrents have no effect, then turn away from them and do not persist in the call to them.

Regarding His saying: **يَوْمَ يَدْعُ الدَّاعِ إِلى شَيْءٍ نُكُرٍ**, al-Raghib said: **Denial (الإنكار)** is the opposite of recognition (العرفان)... its root is for something to come upon the heart which it cannot conceptualize, which is a type of ignorance. The Exalted said: **فَلَمَّا رَأى أَيْدِيَهُمْ لا تَصِلُ إِلَيْهِ نَكِرَهُمْ** — *But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them for strangers.* (11:70). He said: **Dire (النكر)** refers to shrewdness or a difficult matter that is unrecognized. (End of quote).

The speech concluded at His saying **فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ** by explaining their state regarding the profound wisdom presented to them and the deterrents they were reminded of by way of warning. Then, the Glorified repeated a portion of those deterrents, which are news of their state on the Day of Resurrection and the end of the denying nations of the past, in a tone of severe reproach and rebuke that shakes their hearts into awareness and cuts off the roots of their excuses for turning away.

Thus, His saying: **يَوْمَ يَدْعُ الدَّاعِ** etc., is a speech separated from what preceded it to mention the deterrents alluded to earlier, serving as an answer to an assumed question. It is as if when He said: **فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ**, it was asked: "To what will their matter lead?" So it was said: **يَوْمَ يَدْعُ** etc. That is: this is the state of their Hereafter, and that was the end in this world for their predecessors and those like them from the people of Nuh, ‘Ad, Thamud, and others—and they are no better than them.

Based on this, the adverbial phrase (الظرف) in **يَوْمَ يَدْعُ** is connected to what follows in His saying: **يَخْرُجُونَ**. The meaning is: they will emerge from the graves on the day the Summoner summons to a dire thing. Alternatively, it is connected to an elided verb, the estimation being: "Remember the day the Summoner summons," and the result is: "Remember that day and their state therein." The verse carries the same meaning as His saying: **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ** — *Do they await anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them?* (43:66), and His saying: **فَهَلْ يَنْتَظِرُونَ إِلَّا مِثْلَ أَيَّامِ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** — *Do they await anything but the like of the days of those who passed away before them?* (10:102).

The Glorified did not name this Summoner. He has attributed the summoning to Himself in another place in His speech, saying: **يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ** — *The day He will summon you, and you will respond with His praise.* (17:52).

He only brought forth from the news of the Resurrection the news of their being summoned to emerge from the graves and present themselves for the final judgment, and their emergence with downcast eyes, hastening toward the Summoner, to parallel their summons in this world to believe in the signs and their turning away and saying: "An incessant magic."

The meaning of the verse is: Remember the day the Summoner summons to a matter difficult for them, which is the Judgment and the Recompense.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خُشَّعاً أَبْصارُهُمْ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرادٌ مُنْتَشِرٌ** — *with downcast eyes, they will emerge from the graves as if they were scattered locusts,* (54:7). **Downcast (الخشع)** is the plural of *khashi‘*, and humility (الخشوع) is a type of lowliness; it is attributed to the eyes because its manifestation in them is most complete.

**Graves (الأجداث)** is the plural of *jadath*. **Locusts (الجراد)** is a well-known animal. Their comparison in emerging from the graves to scattered locusts is because locusts, in their scattering, enter into one another and mix with one another in different directions; so too will these people be in their emergence from the graves. The Exalted said: **يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ سِراعاً كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ** — *the day they emerge from the graves in haste, as if they were racing toward a target, with their eyes humbled.* (70:43-44).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مُهْطِعِينَ إِلَى الدَّاعِ يَقُولُ الْكافِرُونَ هذا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ** — *hastening toward the Summoner. The faithless will say, ‘This is a hard day!’* (54:8). That is, while they are rushing toward the Summoner, obedient and responding to his call, the disbelievers will say: "This is a hard day," meaning difficult and severe.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ**, he said: "The Resurrection has drawn near—for there is nothing after the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) except the Resurrection—and prophecy and messengership have concluded."

Regarding His saying: **وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ**, [it is narrated] that the Quraysh asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to show them a sign. So he supplicated to Allah, and the moon split into two halves until they looked at it, then it rejoined. They said: "An incessant magic (سحر مستمر)," meaning "continuous."

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with his chain of narration from ‘Ubaydullah ibn ‘Ali, from al-Rida, from his fathers, from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The moon split in Mecca into two pieces. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Bear witness! Bear witness!'"

**I say:** The splitting of the moon for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has been reported in Shi‘i narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) frequently, and their traditionists and scholars have accepted it without hesitation.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* narrated from Anas, who said: "The people of Mecca asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for a sign, so the moon split in Mecca into two parts. Then **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ** was revealed up to His saying **سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ**, meaning 'vanishing'."

In the same source: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi—both in *al-Dala’il*—narrated via Masruq from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "The moon split during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Quraysh said: 'This is the magic of the son of Abu Kabshah.' They said: 'Wait for what the travelers bring you, for Muhammad cannot bewitch all people.' The travelers came and they asked them, and they said: 'Yes, we saw it.' So Allah revealed **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ**."

In the same source: Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim, al-Bayhaqi, and Abu Nu‘aym in *al-Dala’il* narrated via Mujahid from Ibn ‘Umar regarding His saying **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ**: "That was during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—it split into two parts: one part on this side of the mountain and one part behind it. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O Allah, bear witness!'"

In the same source: Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, al-Hakim, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Jubayr ibn Mut‘im regarding His saying **وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ**: "The moon split while we were in Mecca during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) until it became two parts: one part on this mountain and one part on that mountain. The people said: 'Muhammad has bewitched us.' A man said: 'If he has bewitched you, he cannot bewitch all people.'"

In the same source: Ibn Jarir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu‘aym in *al-Dala’il* narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ**: "That occurred before the Migration; the moon split until they saw its two halves."

In the same source: Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, ‘Abdullah ibn Ahmad in *Zawa’id al-Zuhd*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu‘aym narrated from Abu ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Sulami, who said: "Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman addressed us in al-Mada’in. He praised Allah and extolled Him, then said: '**اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ**—Behold, the Hour has drawn near! Behold, the moon split during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)! Behold, the world has announced its departure! Behold, today is the training and tomorrow is the race!'"

**I say:** The splitting of the moon through the supplication of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has been narrated through many different paths from this group of companions: Anas, ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, Ibn ‘Umar, Jubayr ibn Mut‘im, Ibn ‘Abbas, and Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman. In *Ruh al-Ma‘ani*, ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) was also counted among those from whom the Hadith was narrated. Then it was quoted from al-Sayyid al-Sharif in *Sharh al-Mawaqif* and from Ibn al-Subki in *Sharh al-Mukhtasar* that the Hadith is *mutawatir* (متواتر)—mass-transmitted—and its status as such is not doubted. This is the state of the Hadith among the Sunnis, and you have already seen its state among the Shi‘a.

## (A Discourse Summarizing the Matter of the Splitting of the Moon)

The sign of the splitting of the moon (شق القمر) at the hand of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Mecca before the Migration, upon the proposal of the polytheists, is something the Muslims have accepted without doubt.

The Quranic text points to it with manifest indication in His saying: **اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ وَإِنْ يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَيَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ** (54:1-2). The second verse insists that the meaning of **وَانْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ** is a sign that occurred near the time of revelation, which the polytheists turned away from just as they turned away from other signs, saying: "An incessant magic."

It is also supported by widespread and numerous narrations (روايات مستفيضة متكاثرة) reported by both sects and accepted by traditionists, examples of which were provided in the tradition-based discussion.

Thus, the Book and the Sunnah point to it. The splitting of one of the celestial spheres is possible in itself; there is no evidence for its rational impossibility. The occurrence of supernatural events (الحوادث الخارقة للعادة)—among which are miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزات)—is permissible. We have previously provided a detailed discussion in the first volume of this book regarding their possibility and occurrence, with the Quran being the clearest witness. Therefore, it is obligatory to accept this sign, even if it is not among the essential necessities of the religion (ضروريات الدين).

An objection was raised against it: that the issuance of a miraculous sign from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) upon the proposal of the people contradicts His saying: **وَما نُرْسِلُ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفاً** — *We do not send the signs except as a deterrent.* (17:59). The meaning of the verse is either: "We do not send signs to this nation because the previous nations denied them and these people are similar to them in nature and would deny them, and there is no benefit in sending them when no effect follows," or the meaning is: "We do not send them because We sent them to their predecessors who denied them and were thus punished and destroyed; if We sent them to these people, they would deny them and be punished with an extirpating punishment (عذاب الاستئصال), but We do not wish to hasten their punishment." In any case, signs are not sent to this nation as they were sent to the past nations.

Yes, this applies to signs sent upon the proposal of the people, not to signs that support the messengership, such as the Quran which supports the messengership of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or the signs of the staff and the hand for Musa (عليه‌السلام), and the sign of reviving the dead and others for ‘Isa (عليه‌السلام). Similarly, it does not apply to signs revealed as a grace (لطف) from the Glorified, such as the supernatural acts issued from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) not upon their proposal.

Similar to the previous verse is His saying: **وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً** — *They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth for us from the ground...’* until He said: **قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً** — *Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human apostle?’* (17:90-93), and other such verses.

The answer to this objection requires a preliminary point: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sent as a messenger to all the people of the world with a final prophecy, as indicated by His saying: **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً** — *Say, ‘O mankind! I am indeed the Apostle of Allah to you all...’* (7:158), and His saying: **وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ** — *...this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach.* (6:19), and His saying: **وَلكِنْ رَسُولَ اللهِ وَخاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ** — *...but [he is] the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets.* (33:40), among other verses.

He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) began while in Mecca by calling his people from the inhabitants of Mecca and its surroundings. They met him with all the opposition, harm, and mockery they could muster, and they plotted to expel him, imprison him, or kill him, until his Lord commanded him to migrate. However, a large group of them believed in him while he was in Mecca, even if the majority remained in disbelief. Though the believers were few compared to the polytheists and were oppressed and persecuted, they were nonetheless a group of significant number, as indicated by His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ** — *Have you not regarded those to whom it was said, ‘Keep your hands off [from fighting], and maintain the prayer...’* (4:77). They had asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for permission to fight the polytheists, but Allah did not permit them to do so, as reported regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse. This indicates they were a group with some strength and numbers, and they continued to increase.

Then he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated to Medina and expanded the call there, spreading Islam within it, its surroundings, among the tribes, in Yemen, and other regions of the Peninsula except for Mecca and its surroundings. Then he expanded the call beyond the Peninsula, writing to the kings and leaders of Persia, Rome, and Egypt in the sixth year of the Migration. Then he conquered Mecca in the eighth year of the Migration, and between the Migration and the Conquest, a group of its people and surroundings had embraced Islam.

Then he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed away, and the spread of Islam continued as it did. Islam has never ceased to increase in numbers and spread in fame until our day, reaching one-fifth of the earth's population.

Once this is established, we say: The sign of the splitting of the moon was a proposed sign (آية اقتراحية) that would entail punishment if they denied it. They did deny it and said, "An incessant magic." However, Allah would not destroy all those to whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sent—who are all the people of the earth—because the proof (الحجة) had not been fully established for them at that time. The splitting occurred in the fifth year before the Migration, and He has said: **لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ** — *...so that he who perishes might perish by a manifest proof...* (8:42).

Nor would Allah destroy all the people of Mecca and its surroundings specifically, while there was a group of Muslims among them, as He said: **وَلَوْ لا رِجالٌ مُؤْمِنُونَ وَنِساءٌ مُؤْمِناتٌ لَمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَنْ تَطَؤُهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُمْ مِنْهُمْ مَعَرَّةٌ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ لِيُدْخِلَ اللهُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا لَعَذَّبْنَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً** — *...And were it not for [certain] faithful men and faithful women, whom you did not know—lest you should trample them, and thus some blame should fall on you on their account unknowingly, so that Allah may admit into His mercy whomever He wishes—had they been separate, We would have surely punished the faithless among them with a painful punishment.* (48:25).

Nor would Allah the Glorified save the believers and destroy their disbelievers when a large group of them believed between the fifth year before the Migration and the eighth year after the Migration (the year of the Conquest of Mecca), and then the majority of them believed on the day of the Conquest, and Islam was content with their outward testimony (الشهادتين).

Furthermore, the general population of Mecca and its surroundings were not people of stubbornness and rejection (جحود); rather, the people of rejection and stubbornness were their leaders and chieftains who mocked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), tortured the believers, and proposed signs. It is regarding them that the Exalted says: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — *As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith.* (2:6). Allah promised these rejecters and proposers the prohibition of faith and destruction in several places in His speech; they did not believe, and Allah destroyed them on the day of Badr, and the word of the Lord was fulfilled in truth and justice.

As for relying on His saying: **وَما مَنَعَنا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ** — *Nothing kept Us from sending the signs except that the former peoples denied them.* (17:59) to negate the sending of signs absolutely, the verse certainly does not include the signs that support the messengership, such as the Quran. Nor does it include the signs revealed as a grace, such as the supernatural acts issued from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) like informing of the unseen (المغيبات) and healing the sick through his supplication.

If it were absolute, it would only include the proposed signs and signify that Allah the Glorified did not send the signs that the Quraysh proposed—or did not send the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the signs they proposed—because the previous nations denied them, and the nature of these proposers is the same as theirs; they would deny them, and the consequence would be the descent of punishment, and Allah does not wish to punish them immediately.

The Glorified clarified the reason for not hastening their punishment by saying: **وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ** — *But Allah will not punish them while you are among them, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness.* (8:33). It thus became clear that the impediment to their punishment was the presence of the Messenger among them, as is also indicated by His saying: **وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْها وَإِذاً لا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلافَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً** — *And they were about to unsettle you from the land to expel you from it, and then they would not have stayed after you but for a little while.* (17:76).

Then the Exalted said: **وَما لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللهُ وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ وَما كانُوا أَوْلِياءَهُ إِنْ أَوْلِياؤُهُ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقُونَ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ وَما كانَ صَلاتُهُمْ عِنْدَ الْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكاءً وَتَصْدِيَةً فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ** — *What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them, when they bar [the faithful] from the Holy Mosque, and they are not its custodians? Its custodians are only the God-conscious, but most of them do not know. Their prayer at the House is nothing but whistling and clapping. So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve.* (8:34-35). These verses were revealed following the Battle of Badr.

The verses clarify that there was no impediment on their part to the descent of punishment other than the presence of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) among them. Once the impediment was removed by his departure from among them, they were to taste the punishment, which was the massive slaughter that befell them at the event of Badr.

In summary, the impediment to sending the signs was the denial of the former peoples and the current people's similarity to them in the characteristic of denial, combined with the presence of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) among them which prevented immediate punishment. When a cause for punishment existed—such as barring people from the Mosque, whistling, and clapping—and one of the two pillars of the impediment was removed (his presence among them), there was no longer an impediment to the punishment, nor to the descent and sending of the sign so that the word might be fulfilled against them and they be punished due to their denial of it and due to other causes like barring people from the Mosque.

It follows that His saying: **وَما مَنَعَنا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ** etc., only signifies the withholding of sending signs as long as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them. As for sending them and delaying the punishment until his departure from among them, there is no indication in the verse against it. Indeed, the Glorified explicitly stated that the event of Badr was a sign (آية) and what befell them therein was a punishment. Likewise, if the meaning of the verse were the refusal to send signs because it would be futile due to their being predisposed to denial, then sending them while delaying the punishment and exemplary lesson (النكال) until the departure of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from among them would serve the purpose of Allah establishing the truth and nullifying falsehood. Thus, let the sign of the splitting of the moon be among the revealed signs whose benefit was the descent of punishment upon them after the departure of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from among them.

As for His saying: **قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً**, its meaning is not the negation of supporting the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with miraculous signs, nor the denial of their descent in principle. How could it be so? He negates it from himself insofar as he is a "human apostle." If that were the intended meaning, it would imply the denial of the miracles of all prophets, as each of them was a human apostle. The explicit text of the Quran regarding the stories of the prophets and their signs contradicts that. Most clearly of all, the very verse itself—being from the Quran which challenges others with its miraculousness (الإعجاز)—contradicts such a notion.

Rather, its meaning is that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a human apostle who, of himself, is not capable of any of the signs they propose to him. The matter belongs only to Allah the Glorified; if He wills, He sends them, and if He does not will, He does not. The Exalted said: **وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لَئِنْ جاءَتْهُمْ آيَةٌ لَيُؤْمِنُنَّ بِها قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَما يُشْعِرُكُمْ أَنَّها إِذا جاءَتْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — *They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths that if a sign comes to them they will surely believe in it. Say, ‘The signs are only with Allah,’ and what will make you realize that even if it comes they will not believe?* (6:109). He said, narrating from the people of Nuh: **قالُوا يا نُوحُ قَدْ جادَلْتَنا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدالَنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ قالَ إِنَّما يَأْتِيكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ إِنْ شاءَ** — *They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us and you have disputed with us much. Now bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’ He said, ‘Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes...’* (11:32-33). And He said: **وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** — *...It is not for any apostle to bring a sign except by Allah’s leave.* (40:78). The verses in this sense are many.

Among the objections to the sign of the splitting is the claim that if the moon had split as is said, all people would have seen it and it would have been recorded by astronomers in the East and the West, as it would be among the most wondrous of celestial signs. No parallel has been found in the history and books researching celestial conditions that have reached us, despite the strong motives to hear of and transmit such an event.

The response is summarized as follows: First, it is possible that it was overlooked; there is no evidence that every earthly or celestial event must be known to people and preserved by them, inherited from one generation to the next.

Second, the Hijaz and the surrounding Arab lands and others did not have observatories for celestial conditions. Observatories existed in India and the West among the Romans, Greeks, and others, and it has not been proven that an observatory existed at that time—which, according to some narrations, was the beginning of the fourteenth night of Dhu al-Hijjah, five years before the Migration.

Furthermore, the Western lands that were concerned with this matter have a difference in horizon (اختلاف الأفق) from Mecca that necessitates a significant time difference. The moon—according to some narrations—was full and split around sunset when it was rising, and it remained split for only a short time before rejoining. Thus, its rising over the Western lands would have occurred after it had already rejoined.

Moreover, we suspect non-Muslims among the followers of the Church and idolatry in religious matters that have a beneficial connection to Islam.

Another objection is the claim that splitting only occurs by the cessation of attraction (التجاذب) between the two halves, in which case rejoining would be impossible. Thus, if it had split, it would never have rejoined.

The answer is that rational impossibility (الاستحالة العقلية) is denied. As for habitual impossibility (الاستحالة العادية)—in the sense of breaking the norm—if it were to prevent rejoining after splitting, it would have first prevented splitting after being joined. Yet it did not prevent it, and the entire discussion is based on the permissibility of the supernatural (خرق العادة).
